feed.title: Gnarly Void - fiber
feed.subtitle: Tuning the Silence Into A Harmony of Matter

entry.title: Bowlhaus I
entry.content:
A study in form, balance, and bold reduction.

This Bauhaus-inspired owl distills the subject down to its essential geometry - circles, arcs, and color blocks arranged with intention. The symmetry is almost mechanical, but the oversized eyes and softened edges keep it firmly in the realm of character.

Primary colors anchor the composition against a deep black field, giving it that classic Bauhaus tension: function versus expression, system versus personality. The eyes pull you in immediately - equal parts alert, curious, and slightly surreal.

Tufted with thick, high-pile texture, the rigid geometry breaks just enough at the edges to remind you this is still fiber, still handmade. Precision meets softness.

Bowlhaus sits somewhere between mascot and modernist study - a minimal owl that feels both engineered and alive.

entry.title: A 3 foot tufted stick of RAM
entry.content:
A tufted textile recreation of a computer RAM stick, crafted with surprising technical accuracy. The piece features a green circuit board base with yellow contact pins running along the bottom edge. The memory chips are represented by alternating rectangles in cream, navy blue, and light mint colors, arranged in the characteristic symmetric pattern of RAM modules. Yellow dots above the chips mimic capacitors or other surface-mount components. Two circular elements cap each end--one in bright yellow, one in navy blue--representing mounting hardware or heat spreaders.

The attention to detail extends to the actual layout: the chips and components are positioned where they'd actually appear on a real DDR4 or DDR5 module. Made with short cut pile on a green tufted background.

This piece takes something typically invisible and hyper-abstract (system memory) and forces it into the physical world at an exaggerated scale. A stick of RAM, but oversized, tactile, and slow.

It's a nod to the infrastructure layer that underpins everything we're building right now. Models, agents, orchestration layers - all of it ultimately collapses down into memory, allocation, and throughput. This rug turns that hidden dependency into something you can actually see, touch, and trip over.

There's a bit of humor in it too. RAM is expensive, so naturally the solution is to tuft your own. But beneath that is a quieter idea: we're increasingly surrounded by systems we don't physically interact with anymore. This piece pulls one of those systems back into human scale.

A tribute, or maybe an offering, to the machines.

Process

Materials

entry.title: A wavy, blue, framebreaking tufted fiber piece
entry.content: A study in movement and restraint, this tufted piece uses a sequence of flowing, offset waves to create depth without relying on hard contrast. The palette moves from deep cobalt through pale mint into soft seafoam and gray, evoking water, erosion, and layered natural forms. Its irregular silhouette breaks away from the traditional rectangular rug, allowing the composition to expand outward and feel uncontained. The result is a tactile, sculptural surface that shifts with light and perspective - somewhere between landscape and motion captured in fiber.

entry.title: Not a Nott I
entry.content:
A geometric study in wool inspired by the textile compositions of David Nott. Blocks, arcs, and circles interlock like architectural elements, forming a small landscape of structure and balance.

Not a Nott is a geometric tufted composition built from circles, arcs, and modular blocks arranged within a heavy black grid.

Bold outlines divide color and texture into architectural shapes. Rust, sand, ochre, and stone tones create a warm, grounded palette while the varying pile directions and densities give the surface a tactile depth.

The composition feels structured but playful - part stained glass window, part abstract map, part mechanical diagram. Circles anchor the design while rectangular forms create rhythm and movement throughout the frame.

It is a nod to the influence, but intentionally not a reproduction.

Not a Nott.

entry.title: Drippy, fluid, and carved green fiber piece
entry.content:
This hand-tufted rug explores organic movement through layered green tones and sculpted pile. Deep forest green "drips" cascade over lighter mint and cream shapes, creating a fluid, melting effect that feels both natural and surreal. The carved texture enhances the dimensional look, giving the piece a soft topographic quality where colors appear to flow into one another.

Multiple shades of green create depth and contrast, while the high-low tufting and hand carving emphasize the dripping forms. The result is a sculptural textile that blurs the line between rug and wall art.

Inspired by liquid motion, mossy landscapes, and experimental fiber design, this piece works equally well as a small statement rug or as textured fiber art for a wall.

entry.title: Damn Zippy inspired bird tattoo fiber art
entry.content: Inspired by more tattoo art, this piece features a colorful bird. It was originally drawn and tattooed by Olive aka Damn Zippy. Check her out here

entry.title: Traditional tattoo inspired flower fiber piece
entry.content:
A hand-tufted rug inspired by traditional tattoo flash.

This piece features a bold five-petal flower with a bright yellow center, saturated red petals, and heavy black outlines. The white halo gives the design breathing room, while the deep green leaves ground it with contrast and balance.

Built with thick pile and sharp color blocking, it's designed to read clearly from a distance and feel plush up close. The sculpted silhouette follows the leaves and petals, turning the graphic into a functional object.

Classic flash energy, translated into fiber.

entry.title: Abstract Dreamsicle
entry.content:
Exploring softness as structure.

Layered pastel forms drift and overlap, anchored by a dense, warm base that grounds the composition. Rounded protrusions interrupt the surface like half-remembered details, inviting touch as much as inspection.

I spent extra time trimming and shearing this one. I wanted the layers to look like they were on top of each other and had "depth".

entry.title: Yarn Wall
entry.content:
Between weaving, crocheting, and now tufting, we have a decent collection of yarn going. We had been storing it stacked up in a tight little Ikea shelf but that made it hard to sort and see what we had. I saw this idea online and thought it was great because it both functions and looks good. This doesn't quite cover quite all the fiber supply but it handles a lot of it. We are considering one more board on the wall adjacent the right board.

Process

First, I built some simple frames out of 1x2s. I did this to give the pegboard some "depth" to allow for the pegs to stay in place. I could have used nylon spacers but went this route instead. After that, I screwed the pegboards on top of this frame. Then I flipped it over and attached 3 tooth hangers on each. I hung these using drywall anchors because there was no stud perfectly where we wanted it. With 3 hangers spreading out the load, I'm confident it should stay put.

Materials

  • 1x2s
  • pegboards - (2) 2' x 4'
  • screws
    • #8 - 1 1/2" (for the frame)
    • #6 - 1" (attaching pegboard to frame)

entry.title: NYC MTA G Train fiber piece
entry.content:
This piece is dedicated to the G train in New York City.

The G is an odd line. It does not touch Manhattan. It runs quietly through Brooklyn and Queens, connecting places that feel adjacent but rarely centered. It is often overlooked, often delayed, and deeply relied on by the people who use it every day.

Rendered in soft fiber, the familiar green circle becomes tactile and slow. What is usually signage and schedule turns into something physical - dense, imperfect, and worn at the edges. The letterform holds its shape, but only just. The pile absorbs sharpness. The symbol becomes an object.

Like the train itself, this piece is about persistence rather than spectacle. About movement that happens off to the side. About systems that are lived in more than they are noticed.

A transit mark translated into texture.

entry.title: Color Experiment #1
entry.content: What started out as an experiment with a random yarn color I found turned into a fiber piece with a blast of color that I love. It gives me 90s psychadelic vibes.

entry.title: A Tribute to My Lord and Savior, Vyvanse
entry.content: A blue and white capsule inspired by Vyvanse. I was diagnosed later in life and this medicine has improved my life in ways I couldn't imagine. I used to think I was just lazy or apathethic, but turns out my brain just functions differently.

entry.title: King Gizzard and the Lizard Wizard Alligator Fiber Art
entry.content:
Process

I'm a big fan of King Gizz and wanted to create a piece inspired by them. The alligator is available in a simple 2d outline that was perfect for tufting. I decided to mount it on a thin piece of birch that I painted bright purple. I got the paint in the samples section for $2 and I think it provides a perfect backdrop.

This is an early piece so there are still some rough edges. The top of the back could have been a thicker outline. I also chose not to give it pupils as I didn't think it would look very good as a small dot of yarn.

Materials

Yarn

  • acrylic

Wood

  • birch

entry.title: Geometric Color Block Hand-tufted Rug
entry.content:
Process

Started this as a wall hanging. Tufted the top left black square first, then added the center squares. Halfway through I got a new long pile tufting machine and decided to pivot it into a rug instead. You can see the longer pile in the beige corners - that's where I started using the new machine. The rest is shorter pile from the original wall hanging plan.

Materials

Yarn:

  • Acrylic
  • Wool

entry.title: Vulfmon-inspired Black, Silver, Gold, and White Wall Weaving
entry.content:
Inspired by a piece seen in a Vulfmon video

Process

Materials

entry.title: Mondrian-esque Purple, Gold, and Black Hand-tufted Rug
entry.content: My first rug. I recently got a tufting machine and wanted to start simple. The black square came first, then I decided to add a few rows of purple. After that, I decided a gold border would look good.
